disengaged-employee.title: The Disengaged Employee
disengaged-employee.description: Alex, a previously high-performing team member, has become withdrawn. They miss meetings, ship late, and barely speak up. As their manager, you need to have a conversation.

persona.name: Alex
persona.context: For the past six weeks, Alex has gone from your most reliable analyst to your most quiet. Their Jira tickets are slipping. They sit through stand-ups with their camera off. You've just scheduled a 1:1 — they're now sitting across from you. They look tired. How do you open?

learningObjectives: Use curiosity over judgment to open difficult conversations
Avoid the 'fix-it' reflex when an employee needs to be heard
Close with commitments both parties own

open.speaker: narrator
open.message: Alex sits down. Small smile, shoulders tight. The room is quiet. You have one chance to set the tone of this conversation.

choices.text: "Hey Alex — thanks for making time. I've noticed things feel a bit different lately and I wanted to check in. How are you doing, honestly?"
choices.feedback: Strong opening. You named what you observed without accusing, and you invited honesty. This is how trust gets built.

choices.text: "Alex, your delivery has dropped this quarter. I need to understand what's going on and what you plan to do about it."
choices.feedback: This opener frames Alex as the problem to be solved. It may get compliance, but it rarely gets truth.

choices.text: "So — busy week? Anyway, let's run through the sprint status."
choices.feedback: Dodging the real conversation signals you either don't see the issue or don't want to deal with it. Both are a problem.

alex-opens-up.speaker: character
alex-opens-up.message: Alex exhales. "Honestly? I feel invisible here. I've been grinding for two years and every time I raise my hand for the bigger projects, they go to someone else. I'm not sure this is where I should be anymore."

choices.text: "That sounds really frustrating. Tell me more — which moments stand out for you?"
choices.feedback: Excellent. You validated the feeling AND invited specifics. Feelings without facts are hard to act on; this question gets you both.

choices.text: "I hear you, but those projects went to people with more experience in that area. It's not personal."
choices.feedback: Even if factually true, leading with a defense tells Alex their experience of the situation is wrong. You've closed the door they just opened.

choices.text: "Okay — I'll talk to HR about a development plan for you. Let's get you on the next big project."
choices.feedback: You went straight to solutions before understanding the problem. Alex may accept it, but the underlying trust issue is still there.

alex-defensive.speaker: character
alex-defensive.message: Alex crosses their arms. "I'm doing the best I can. The priorities keep changing and nobody tells me anything until it's late. If you want to write me up, just do it."

choices.text: "I'm sorry — I came in too hard. I'm not trying to write you up. I'm trying to understand what's going on, because the Alex I worked with a year ago isn't who I'm seeing right now."
choices.feedback: A real recovery. Naming the misstep and restating your intent can un-escalate. Many managers can't do this — it's a genuine skill.

choices.text: "This isn't about writing you up, it's about results. I need to see different numbers next month."
choices.feedback: Doubling down on performance language confirms Alex's fear that this is a pre-PIP conversation. Walls go up. Information goes dark.

alex-shuts-down.speaker: character
alex-shuts-down.message: Alex gives a flat "sure" and walks you through the sprint in a monotone. Nothing is wrong. Nothing is right. You leave the room knowing less than when you walked in.
alex-shuts-down.feedback: You avoided the conversation that needed to happen. Alex will likely either disengage further or leave — and you won't know why until the exit interview. When the human signal is loud, the sprint board can wait.

alex-growth.speaker: character
alex-growth.message: Alex leans in a little. "The Meridian project — I pitched an angle for it and heard nothing back. Two weeks later Jordan got it. I don't need to win every time, but I'd like to know what I should be doing differently, or if there's even a path here for me."

choices.text: "That feedback loop failed and that's on me — I should have closed it with you either way. Let's spend the rest of this time mapping out what growth looks like for you, and I'll commit to specific check-ins."
choices.feedback: You owned the gap, validated their agency, and moved to co-created next steps. This is the conversation repairing itself.

choices.text: "I hear you. Don't worry — I'll make sure you get the next big one."
choices.feedback: Promises you can't guarantee feel good in the moment and hurt later. If the 'next big one' goes elsewhere for legitimate reasons, you've broken trust again.

choices.text: "I can't promise you the next project. What I can promise is that you'll know where you stand, and we'll build a real plan together. Would that help?"
choices.feedback: Boundary + commitment is underrated. You didn't overpromise and you didn't dodge. This is usually the most sustainable answer.

alex-withdraws.speaker: character
alex-withdraws.message: Alex nods slowly. "Right. Okay." They look at the clock. The energy is gone from the room.

choices.text: "Wait — I just pushed back instead of listening. Can we rewind? What I actually want to know is what you're seeing that I'm not."
choices.feedback: Not a full repair, but a meaningful attempt. Alex may not fully open back up today, but you've shown you can self-correct — and that matters more over time than being right in the moment.

choices.text: "Anyway — let's keep an eye on things and circle back next week."
choices.feedback: You closed the conversation without closing the loop. Alex now has evidence that speaking up doesn't change anything.

alex-lukewarm.speaker: character
alex-lukewarm.message: Alex nods. "Okay. Thanks, I guess." They agree to the plan, but their expression hasn't really changed.
alex-lukewarm.feedback: You moved into solution mode too fast. The development plan might help, but you skipped the step where Alex felt heard — which is usually the real currency. Watch for this 'fix-it reflex' in future conversations: the urge to resolve often serves the manager, not the employee.

ending-success.speaker: narrator
ending-success.message: Alex stands up with real energy for the first time in weeks. "Thanks — this helped more than I expected. Same time next week?"
ending-success.feedback: You did the hard work: curiosity first, defense never, ownership where it was yours, and a plan you can actually both keep. This is what great 1:1s look like. The proof is in the follow-through — but you've earned the right to have it.

ending-partial.speaker: narrator
ending-partial.message: The conversation ends on steadier ground than it started, but the air isn't fully clear. Alex will give it another week before deciding what they really think.
ending-partial.feedback: You recovered from a rough moment — which is more than most managers do. Next time, try to lead with curiosity before defense. Alex is still watching to see if you meant what you said.

ending-failure.speaker: narrator
ending-failure.message: Alex leaves the room looking exactly like they walked in — but quieter. Their next move is probably LinkedIn.
ending-failure.feedback: This conversation reinforced every concern Alex already had. The goal of a disengagement 1:1 is not to assert your authority — it's to make reality speakable. When in doubt, ask one more question before offering one more answer.
